entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
Students play musical games to notice rhythm and melody.

This topic is included in the following course(s): Music: Intro

Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

Sol-fa: Musical Games
☐ Bookmark the Musical Games Playlist (in Quick Links), which includes many selections from the term.

blocks.text:
• Connect each lesson to the previous one for that topic. 

• For music appreciation study, a recap might look like a brief discussion about the name or sound of the piece or instrument you listened to previously. 

• For hymns and folk songs, this might be an effort to hum the melody or recite the words of the song you're currently learning.

Prepare to listen to the day's musical selection or activity by inviting students to consider an interesting musical or historical idea in the lesson, perhaps a unique word, rhythm, instrument, or piece of historical background (usually included in lesson plans).

• Listen to the day's musical selection or practice the activity. 

• Music Appreciation lessons will usually have you listen to a piece of music for 3-8 minutes. If students have a difficult time listening for the duration of the lesson, stop the music at their limit.

• Movement is always a great way to encourage students to engage with the music. Sometimes the lessons will provide a suggestion, but students may make up their own using their whole bodies or creating finger plays. They may also draw, color, or act out a dance with stuffed animals, dolls, or other figures.

• Hymn, folk song, or musical games will sometimes have you listen to a part of the song and then practice echoing back the melody and/or words.
Let students share any thoughts or connections they have about the lesson.

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Moving and Music

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
“Here We Go Round the Mulberry Bush.” Follow the movement directions, ensuring that larger groups or students with spatial perception differences have enough room.
∞ Video Link: Here We Go Round the Mulberry Bush

➜ SING
Now try singing the song and make up some new actions (possibilities: “this is the way we clap our hands,” “sweep the floor,” or “dance around”).
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Along with the lesson's songs, read stories and/or listen to songs about Winnie the Pooh.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Pooh Songs

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
Join-in Rhymes

PREP: Print out and read over instructions.
∞ Link: "The Owl" and "Here We Go"

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Read “The Owl” and “Here We Go” aloud and tell the students what they are going to say with you (the italicized parts). You may want to read the poem aloud first and then have the students join in the second time. Tell the students to copy you closely. Exaggerate louds, softs, ascending, descending tones of your voice.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Along with the lesson's songs, read stories and/or listen to songs about Winnie the Pooh.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Pooh Songs

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
Acting

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Today's songs need your help. In the first one, you need to pretend to be a teapot, and for the second one, you're going to make a lot of animal noises.
∞ Audio Link: I'm a Little Teapot
∞ Audio Link: Old MacDonald Had a Farm

➜ SING
With any extra lesson time, make up some new verses about other animals to “Old MacDonald Had a Farm."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Along with the lesson's songs, read stories and/or listen to songs about Winnie the Pooh.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Pooh Songs

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
Improvising

➜ RECAP
Sing as much of “I'm a Little Teapot” or “Old MacDonald Had a Farm” as you can without the music.

➜ SING
Sing along with the songs today. Make up verses or add any you made up last time.
∞ Audio Link: I'm a Little Teapot
∞ Audio Link: Old MacDonald Had a Farm
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Along with the lesson's songs, read stories and/or listen to songs about Winnie the Pooh.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Pooh Songs

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
Dwarfs and Giants

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Listen to "In the Hall..." by Edvard Grieg. Tiptoe with hands on hips when the music is soft. When it is loud, pretend to be a giant and march with your hands high in the air. During the second song, stand in one place, but move your body up and down as the music gets louder or softer. You can also swing your arms and twist with the music. Ensure that larger groups or students with spatial perception differences have enough room.
∞ Audio Link: In the Hall of the Mountain King
∞ Audio Link: Morning Mood
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Along with the lesson's songs, read stories and/or listen to songs about Winnie the Pooh.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Pooh Songs

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
Skipping

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Today we're going to have fun skipping! (Teacher should demonstrate how to skip to make sure students know how; if skipping is too challenging, teachers can model a shuffle instead.) Listen to “Skip to My Lou” and practice skipping with the music.
∞ Audio Link: Skip to My Lou
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Along with the lesson's songs, read stories and/or listen to songs about Winnie the Pooh.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Pooh Songs

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Skipping Part 2

➜ RECAP
Skip in a circle. If skipping is too challenging, students may shuffle instead.

➜ PRACTICE
We're going to skip more today, but this time, see if you can skip sideways! Try sideways skipping (left and right). (If there is a group of students, have them take hands and skip sideways in a ring.) Try to make up another way to skip (maybe backwards!). After you feel confident, skip and sing with the music.
∞ Audio Link: Skip to My Lou
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Along with the lesson's songs, read stories and/or listen to songs about Winnie the Pooh.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Pooh Songs

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
Guessing Instruments

PREP: Think of how you want to imitate different instruments for your student to guess.

➜ PRACTICE
Imitate various instruments and have your students guess what they are. If students find this excessively difficult, do it together or make it a funny game of charades.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Along with the lesson's songs, read stories and/or listen to songs about Winnie the Pooh.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Pooh Songs

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 9
lessons.body:
Echoing

➜ SING
You get to echo the singer in this song. (Sing with the kids in the audio.) Sing the song through a few times.
∞ Audio Link: My Aunt Came Back 

➜ DISCUSS
Where are some of the places the aunt went? Look them up on your map. (Click on countries or islands to zoom in to find the cities/places mentioned in the song.)
∞ Map Link: Clickable World Map
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Along with the lesson's songs, read stories and/or listen to songs about Winnie the Pooh.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Pooh Songs

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 10
lessons.body:
“My Aunt Came Back” (cont.)

➜ RECAP
Keep practicing “My Aunt Came Back.” Can you hum the tune without the music?

➜ SING
Sing the song through a few times with the audio, then try singing it on your own.
∞ Audio Link: My Aunt Came Back
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Along with the lesson's songs, read stories and/or listen to songs about Winnie the Pooh.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Pooh Songs

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 11
lessons.body:
Walking to a Beat

➜ PRACTICE
Can you walk at a very steady speed? Try to take steps so that your foot hits the ground with every tap of the stick in this song--you may want to play it more than once. If you can't walk with the taps perfectly, don’t worry. Some things take practice. After you're done walking, listen to the song again and see if you can learn the words.
∞ Audio Link: Hickory Dickory Dock
(Note: This song is on Spotify. You will need a free account to listen to it.)
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Along with the lesson's songs, read stories and/or listen to songs about Winnie the Pooh.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Pooh Songs

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 13
lessons.body:
Meowing

➜ INTRO
Listen to these two cats. I bet they can “meow” better than any other cats you've ever heard.
∞ Audio Link: Duet for Two Cats

➜ PRACTICE
Now you meow too!
∞ Audio Link: Meow Game
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and “Wee Sing Songs."
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Children's Songs & Fingerplays
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Around the World

lessons.body:
Nutcracker and Imitating

➜ VIEW & PRACTICE
Start watching the Nutcracker Ballet, and try to imitate the dancers’ moves.
∞ Video Link: Nutcracker Ballet
lessons.teacherNotes:

Songs from the lesson and “Wee Sing Songs."

Keep watching the Nutcracker Ballet and try to imitate the dancers’ moves.

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 16
lessons.body:
Guessing Melodies

PREP: Select several songs/melodies with which your child is familiar. (Pick some that the student will know quickly and some that are harder to recognize.)

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Play each melody and ask the student to guess the melody. Intersperse lesser-known pieces with the more familiar ones until lesson time is over.
∞ Audio Link: Musical Games Playlist
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and “Wee Sing Songs."
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Children's Songs & Fingerplays
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Around the World

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 17
lessons.body:
Fingerplay

➜PREP & PRACTICE
Learn this fingerplay song, and then teach it to your students during the lesson.
∞ Video Link: Open, Shut Them
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and “Wee Sing Songs."
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Children's Songs & Fingerplays
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Around the World

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 18
lessons.body:
Clapping and Marching in Time
⍞ Materials: Item to balance on head while marching

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Balance item on your head while doing the following:
• Clap with the music.
• Walk with the music.
• March with the music (lifting knees).
• Turn with directions (if the student knows directions, the teacher may
  call out “about right,” “about left,” etc.)
∞ Audio Link: Farewell to a Slavic Woman
∞ Audio Link: Pomp and Circumstance
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and “Wee Sing Songs."
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Children's Songs & Fingerplays
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Around the World

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 19
lessons.body:
Singing and Imitating

➜ SING
Today we're going to start learning a song called “Over in the Meadow.” Imitate each of the animals that the singer describes. Listen to the song again and sing along where you can.
∞ Audio Link: Over in the Meadow

➜ DISCUSS
Do you think there should be more animals in the meadow? What would they be?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and “Wee Sing Songs."
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Children's Songs & Fingerplays
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Around the World

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 20
lessons.body:
“Over in the Meadow” (cont.)

➜ RECAP
Hum/sing as much of “Over in the Meadow” as you can.

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Listen to and practice the song and imitate the animals; try to remember them all and sing by yourself.
∞ Audio Link: Over in the Meadow
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and “Wee Sing Songs."
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Children's Songs & Fingerplays
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Around the World

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 21
lessons.body:
“Hokey Pokey” Dance

➜ RECAP
Review right and left hands, elbows, etc., with students.

➜ NOTE
Make a circle (large enough for all students to have plenty of room; an arm's length is a good rule).

➜ DANCE
Follow the directions in the song and put arms, legs, etc., in and out of the circle. When the song says “do the hokey pokey,” students turn around in a circle and clap their hands. Dance with students to demonstrate.
∞ Audio Link: Hokey Pokey
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and “Wee Sing Songs."
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Children's Songs & Fingerplays
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Around the World

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 22
lessons.body:
“Hokey Pokey” Dance (cont.)

➜ RECAP
Do you remember the “Hokey Pokey”? Take a few minutes to do the dance.

➜ NOTE
Make a circle (large enough for all students).
∞ Audio Link: Hokey Pokey

➜ SING
Start learning a new song. First listen, then echo the singer.
∞ Audio Link: Bill Grogan's Goat
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and “Wee Sing Songs."
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Children's Songs & Fingerplays
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Around the World

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 23
lessons.body:
Sing-Along Song

➜ SING
Hum "Bill Grogan's Goat." Then listen and sing along.
∞ Audio Link: Bill Grogan's Goat
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and “Wee Sing Songs."
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Children's Songs & Fingerplays
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Wee Sing Around the World

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
Marching Part 2
⍞ Materials: Item to balance on head while marching

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
As you listen to these marches, lift your knees high like a soldier marching, shoulders back, chest out. 
∞ Audio Link: Washington Post March

➜ VIEW
View soldiers marching. Try swinging your arms while marching and balancing something on your head. If the item keeps falling, students might use a hand to help or try a small, soft item.
∞ Video Link: Soldiers Marching
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and the "Hole in the Bucket" and "Had a Little Rooster" playlists.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Had a Little Rooster
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: There's a Hole in the Bucket

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
Animal Guessing Game

➜ PRACTICE
Imitate different animals and have the students guess which animal you are imitating. Then allow students to act out their own versions of animals for you to guess. 
Suggestions: strut like a turkey, hop like a rabbit, etc.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and the "Hole in the Bucket" and "Had a Little Rooster" playlists.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Had a Little Rooster
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: There's a Hole in the Bucket

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 27
lessons.body:
An Echoing Song

➜ SING
After the singer sings a line, sing it with the children in the recording. Listen several times to catch the words and melody.
∞ Audio Link: Purple Light
(Note: This song is on Spotify. You will need a free account to listen to it.)
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and the "Hole in the Bucket" and "Had a Little Rooster" playlists.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Had a Little Rooster
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: There's a Hole in the Bucket

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 28
lessons.body:
Tongue Twisters

PREP: Select a few tongue twisters to teach your students.
∞ Link: Tongue Twisters

➜ DISCUSS
Explain what a tongue twister is, and then teach your students a few short ones. Speak slowly and clearly. Some students may benefit from repeating line by line; others may benefit from accentuating the rhythm with a 'sing-song' voice.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and the "Hole in the Bucket" and "Had a Little Rooster" playlists.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Had a Little Rooster
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: There's a Hole in the Bucket

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 29
lessons.body:
Moving to ¾ Time

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Listen to some of this collection of tunes. Every time the song changes, use a different movement. 
∞ Audio Link: Waltz Music

➜ NOTE
Teachers should initially direct movements, then let students choose their own after a few tunes. Suggestions: swing arms back and forth, twirl around, pretend to be a conductor, march, tiptoe, etc. Encourage students to listen closely to music and try to make their movements “match” it.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and the "Hole in the Bucket" and "Had a Little Rooster" playlists.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Had a Little Rooster
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: There's a Hole in the Bucket

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 30
lessons.body:
Fingerplay

➜ PRACTICE
We're going to learn a fingerplay called “The Eensy Weensy Spider” today. You may have heard the song before, but did you know that you can use your fingers to act it out?
∞ Video Link: Eensy Weensy Spider Fingerplay
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and the "Hole in the Bucket" and "Had a Little Rooster" playlists.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Had a Little Rooster
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: There's a Hole in the Bucket

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 31
lessons.body:
Spelling and Clapping

➜ SING
We're going to start learning a spelling-and-clapping song today. With each verse, the singer is going to drop a letter of BINGO, the dog’s name in the song. You need to clap or stomp your feet instead of singing the letter. By the end of the song, you'll be doing a lot of stomping and clapping. Listen to the song first, so you get an idea of how it goes.
∞ Video Link: BINGO with Lyrics
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and the "Hole in the Bucket" and "Had a Little Rooster" playlists.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Had a Little Rooster
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: There's a Hole in the Bucket

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 32
lessons.body:
“BINGO” (cont.)

➜ RECAP
Can you sing BINGO from memory?

➜ SING
See if you can sing the song with the video and correctly stomp or clap every dropped letter.
∞ Video Link: BINGO with Lyrics
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and the "Hole in the Bucket" and "Had a Little Rooster" playlists.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Had a Little Rooster
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: There's a Hole in the Bucket

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 33
lessons.body:
Basic Waltz Step

PREP: Use Waltz Step Instructions (see Appendix) to learn and teach the waltz step.

➜ NOTE
If you do this as a group activity in couples, couples should step sideways rather than forward. (The couple should move together, which means one of them will be moving with the opposite foot.)

➜ PRACTICE
Today, we're going to learn a waltz step.
∞ Audio Link: Moon River with Orchestra
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Songs from the lesson and the "Hole in the Bucket" and "Had a Little Rooster" playlists.
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: Had a Little Rooster
∞ Audio Link: Afternoons: There's a Hole in the Bucket

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 34
lessons.body:
Waltz Step (cont.)

➜ NOTE 
Remember that the goal in learning this waltz step is for the student to continue developing his/her attention to rhythm, awareness of space, melody, appreciation of the music, etc., rather than learning to perform it perfectly.

➜ RECAP
Dance the waltz step from the last lesson without music and with only counting, then practice it with the music. 
∞ Audio Link: Moon River with Orchestra
∞ Audio Link: Ashokan's Farewell
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Dance the waltz step with a group of people using the recordings from the lesson.

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 35
lessons.body:
Year Recap/Catch Up

➜ RECAP
Ask students to name one or two of their favorite songs/dances/activities from the year.
Or use this time to make up any missed lessons. 

➜ PRACTICE
Practice a favorite song or dance.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Favorite audios from the term and/or year.

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 36
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course. 

Questions will come from: Musical Games Coursework 

➜ NOTE
Exams are optional for this course as the focus is on enjoyment and fun.
